text: Hello, this is a case of laparoscopic myomectomy. This patient has nine centimeter intramural myoma over the fundal and anterior wall of uterus.
Vasopressin is injected into the tissue plane just above the capsule of the uterus, visible during the laparoscopic myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon injects vasopressin, causing the uterus to turn marble white in appearance, indicating vasoconstriction.
The surgeon makes a crescent-shaped incision on the uterus using the vibrating jaw of the harmonic scalpel, targeting the area affected by the intramural myoma.
A clear surgical plane is achieved, with visible leakage of vasopressin fluid introduced above the capsule during the laparoscopic myomectomy.
Two ampoules are mixed into 200 ml of saline solution, and the myoma screw is prepared for application to the intramural myoma on the fundal and anterior wall of the uterus.
The surgeon pulls the uterus upward and makes a circumferential incision on its anterior wall, using surgical instruments to carefully cut through the tissue.
The surgeon uses the left hand to support the myoma screw while the right hand makes an incision on the uterus. The incisions converge, leaving a portion of the serosa attached to the uterus.
That will give you a good shape
The surgeon lifts the uterus with anterior traction using the left hand while employing a harmonic scalpel and suction irrigation to slowly separate the myoma from the uterine tissue.
If you have a good plane then the separation will be easy.
The surgeon repeatedly adjusts the myoma screw, applying it at the base of the myoma to optimize traction during the procedure.

As eighty percent of the myoma is exposed, the surgeon carefully uses a harmonic device to separate the remaining myoma from the base, avoiding abrupt disruption of the tissue plane to prevent stripping the endometrium.
To prevent bleeding that is important and to prevent stripping of the cavity endometrium.
The surgeon repositions the mama screw on the base of the myoma to optimize traction for continued separation from the uterus.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to separate the remaining attachment of the myoma from the capsule. The sigmoid colon is visible below, requiring careful anterior traction to prevent damage during the separation process.
The surgeon separates the biomass tube and begins suturing the area.
The surgeon continues double-layer intracorporeal continuous suturing using a two-third circle needle and a 30 mm needle with number one Vicryl suture.
The video shows the complete, unedited process of intracorporeal continuous suturing using a two-third circle needle and number one vehicle suture material.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing of the muscle layer, ensuring each stitch is tightened and properly approximated. The suturing technique involves taking the muscle layer first, followed by the serous layer, as the cavity is not open.
The surgeon exits the needle through the serous layer after completing the muscle layer suturing, and continues suturing by taking the serous layer.
The surgeon uses the open jaw of the Maryland dissector to pull and tighten the suture, ensuring proper tension in the continuous suturing of the muscle layer.
The surgeon holds the suture to maintain traction while passing the needle through the tissue. The procedure is performed using three ports, with no fourth port utilized.
Extracorporeal knot is also good for laparoscopic myomectomy. But you can use the intracorporeal as well with the continuous suture.

The surgeon holds the suture continuously with the right hand, taking a bite to maintain tension. The first knot is pulled tight using the open jaw of the Maryland or needle holder, pressing the tissue as the suture is pulled out.
The surgeon performs double-layer continuous suturing, demonstrating the technique suitable for both laparoscopic and robotic myomectomy procedures.

So laparoscopic intracorporeal is also necessary. So this is to practice the robotic surgery. And we do nowadays a lot of robotic myomectomy and unfortunately robot cannot use the extracorporeal. So you have to use intracorporeal suturing also. So this is now complete.
The surgeon cuts the suture and carefully removes it, ensuring that the iliac vessel is not pricked during the handling of the suture.

The surgeon performs suction irrigation to remove blood clots from the surgical field, using copious lavage multiple times to clear the area. Visible extra tissue bulging out is noted, which will dissolve soon.

The surgeon begins the process of morselation on a nine-centimeter myoma, using a morselator to gradually fragment and remove the fibroid tissue. The procedure is visually detailed, showing the fibroid being reduced in size over time.
The surgeon prepares to close the 15mm laparoscopic port used for morselation, ensuring proper closure to prevent hernia formation post-surgery.

So this is a case of laparoscopic myomectomy in which a nine centimeter intramural myoma was removed and the closure of the wound was performed with the intracorporeal suturing.
